title: Your Life as a Samurai at Every Rank (Honor Kills You)
hook: You're a peasant boy with a borrowed spear and no right to touch a blade. Climb the samurai ranks and you'll hold a million koku of rice — and finally understand why honor is the most expensive thing a man can carry, and who really owns the sword at the top.
body: POV: you're a samurai, born a peasant below the line, climbing a real feudal-Japan ranking system one rank of rice at a time. Everything here is measured in KOKU — the rice reckoned to feed one person for a year, the true measure of a samurai's rank, pay and a lord's power. THE ASHIGARU: 0 koku, a borrowed spear, two rice balls a day. THE SAMURAI: ~30 koku and the two swords — the daisho — the visible mark only a samurai could wear. THE MOUNTED RETAINER: ~500 koku, a horse, men who bleed on your word. THE KARO: ~3,000 koku, the lord's right hand — and the order to serve as your own teacher's second. THE DAIMYO: 10,000 koku, the exact line where a retainer becomes a lord. THE GREAT LORD: a million koku, Kaga-scale — while the sword class you bled to enter was never more than 5% of the people, ruling the other 95%. THE SHOGUN'S JAPAN: 4,000,000 koku held directly, ~30,000,000 across the whole country. THE END OF THE SWORD: 1876, when an edict banned wearing swords in public and 300 years of the sword ended not on a battlefield but at a desk and a bank. Real, verified history woven into the story: the 1588 sword hunt that sealed the class line by law; alternate attendance (sankin-kotai) that used a lord's own wealth as a leash; the Dojima Rice Exchange in Osaka, licensed 1730 — one of the world's first futures markets, where merchants with no swords set the price of every samurai's pay; and the 1877 Satsuma Rebellion, the last samurai revolt, crushed by a conscript peasant army with rifles. A dramatized cautionary POV — characters are fictional, the documented history is real. Watch the whole series — every life, every rank, every way the climb costs you. For educational and entertainment purposes.
pinned: Here's the part that stays with me: by the Edo period, merchants — legally the LOWEST of the four classes, below the farmers — held the daimyo's debt, and the price of every samurai's rice-pay was set in a merchant futures market in Osaka (Dojima, licensed 1730). The men with no swords owned the sword. Which rank do you think breaks first — the honor, or the money?
poll: Where does honor finally cost you everything? THE KARO (you behead your own teacher on order) / THE DAIMYO (your family becomes hostages in Edo) / THE GREAT LORD (a merchant owns your debt) / THE END OF THE SWORD (by law, the sword is worth nothing)
